questions.question: Why is "prompt the model to reply in JSON" not enough for a production extraction pipeline?
options: It fails 5-15% of the time even on frontier models — missing braces, wrong types, hallucinated fields, leaked prose
JSON output is against most providers' terms of service
Prompted JSON is always slower than constrained decoding
Models can only emit JSON through tool calls
questions.explanation: Free-form JSON prompting works 85-95% of the time and fails in six characteristic ways, including truncation at the token limit and "Here is your JSON:" prose leaks. Production pipelines need a guarantee, not a probability.

questions.question: What is constrained decoding?
options: Restricting the model to a smaller vocabulary to save memory
Masking tokens at decode time so the model literally cannot emit output that violates the schema
Truncating the output at a fixed token budget
Re-prompting the model until its output validates
questions.explanation: Constrained decoding enforces the schema during generation: invalid next tokens are masked out of the sampling distribution, so the output is guaranteed to parse and validate. Retry-until-valid (approach two) pays for failures; constrained decoding prevents them.

questions.question: Under OpenAI strict mode, which failure mode remains possible?
options: Parse errors from truncated JSON
Schema violations from hallucinated fields
Refusal — the model decides the input cannot fit the schema
All three remain possible
questions.explanation: Strict mode makes parse errors and schema violations impossible via constrained decoding. What remains is refusal — e.g. "the email was a poem, not an invoice" — which your code must handle as a first-class typed outcome, not an exception.

questions.question: What do Pydantic (Python) and Zod (TypeScript) contribute to structured output?
options: They host the model weights locally
They define the schema once in native types and translate it to each provider's format, returning typed, validated objects
They replace JSON with a binary format
They are required by OpenAI for all API calls
questions.explanation: Pydantic's model_json_schema() and Zod's zodResponseFormat() generate JSON Schema from native type definitions, and parse provider output back into typed instances — declare the schema once, enforce it end to end.

questions.question: Why do structured outputs matter especially for small models?
options: Small models emit JSON natively without prompting
A 3B model with grammar enforcement can beat a 70B model with raw prompting on structured tasks — reliability decouples from model size
Constrained decoding only works below 7B parameters
Small models cannot be fine-tuned
questions.explanation: Grammar-based enforcement (outlines, guidance, lm-format-enforcer) masks invalid tokens regardless of model quality, so a tiny model becomes reliable on structured tasks. This decoupling of reliability from size is why structured outputs are a production cornerstone.
